nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef and lagoon environments to approximately 14–30 m depth, particularly among staghorn Acropora and Porites coral formations and coral rubble areas. Two disjunct populations: one in the northern Western Pacific (Ryukyu and Ogasawara Islands of Japan, Philippines) and one in the Rowley Shoals off northwestern Australia (Timor Sea).

diet.notes: Primarily grazes on seaweeds and benthic algae in the wild (trophic level ~2.7). In captivity, has been documented readily accepting dried marine algae (nori sheets on a clip), spirulina-based pellets and flakes, and a variety of fresh or blanched vegetables. Keeping a well-fed individual tends to reduce the risk of coral nipping; supplementing with herbivore-formulated foods and seaweed sheets daily is generally advised.

summary: Peaceful, hardy herbivorous rabbitfish from the Western Pacific, recognised by its bright yellow body, white-and-black head mask, and a distinctive large dark blotch on its upper flanks. Venomous dorsal and anal spines; generally considered reef-safe when well fed.
careNotes: The One Spot Foxface (also sold as Blotched Foxface Rabbitfish) is recognisable by its striking coloration: bright yellow body with the head and front portion being white overlaid by a bold black band running from the mouth to the start of the dorsal fin, plus a large irregular black blotch or eyespot on the centre-to-rear upper flank — the 'one spot' the common name references. Juveniles tend to form large schools, while adults and subadults are typically observed in permanent pairs (including same-sex pairs) in the wild, though in captivity the species is generally kept singly to avoid conspecific aggression with other rabbitfish. IMPORTANT: All dorsal and anal fin spines are venomous (venom chemistry has been found to share similarities with stonefish toxin) and can deliver a painful sting; handle only with a net or aquarium tools, never with bare hands. Reef safety is conditional — a well-fed specimen generally ignores corals, but underfed individuals have been reported to nip at soft corals and LPS polyps; ensuring ample algae is available (nori clips, seaweed sheets) and supplementing with herbivore-formulated pellets is generally advised. Like other rabbitfish, this species can adopt a mottled darker camouflage pattern when stressed, sleeping, or newly introduced — this is normal and not a sign of illness. Temperature sources disagree moderately: LiveAquaria recommends an aquarium range of 72–78°F (22–26°C) while FishBase wild-habitat data indicates 25–29°C; the range recorded here (24–27°C) reflects an average of the two. Reproduction appears to take place around the new moon when large aggregations occur and demersal eggs are laid; captive breeding is not practically achievable. IUCN status is Data Deficient.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is not documented for this species. Like other siganids, it is believed to be a pelagic broadcast spawner with planktonic larvae, with spawning cues tied to lunar cycles (new moon aggregations documented in the wild). These requirements make reliable home-aquarium reproduction essentially infeasible. All trade specimens are generally wild-collected.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source for husbandry parameters: care level moderate, temperament peaceful, reef-safe with caution, herbivore, max size 8 in (~20 cm), min tank 70 US gal (~265 L), temperature 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12. Collection page https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/foxface-rabbitfish listed the species; product page https://www.liveaquaria.com/products/one-spot-foxface provided full parameters.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Siganidae, order Acanthuriformes), maximum size (20.0 cm SL), native range (Ryukyu Islands, Philippines, NW Australia Rowley Shoals), wild temperature range 25.4–29.1°C (mean 28.2°C — warmer than the LiveAquaria hobby recommendation; the recorded range is an average of both), depth 1–30 m, reef-associated coral habitat (Acropora and Porites), diet (seaweeds, trophic level 2.7), venomous stout spines, juvenile schooling behaviour transitioning to adult pairs.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Siganus unimaculatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum size (20 cm TL), two disjunct populations (Ryukyu/Ogasawara/Philippines in the north, Rowley Shoals/Timor Sea in the south), habitat (lagoon and reef to 14 m, Acropora and Porites formations), behaviour (adults and subadults in permanent pairs including same-sex pairs, juveniles in schools), diet (seaweeds, herbivore), reproductive timing (new moon aggregations, demersal eggs), venomous fin spines (chemistry similar to stonefish toxin), IUCN Data Deficient.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic broadcast spawner with planktonic larvae tied to lunar cycles (new moon aggregations); no reliable captive breeding documented. All trade specimens generally wild-collected.
